color_profiles.name: Kashmir Blue Sapphire
color_profiles.cause: Fe2+/Ti4+ IVCT; fine silk inclusions scatter light to produce the famous velvety texture.

color_profiles.name: Burmese / Ceylon Sapphire
color_profiles.cause: Fe2+/Ti4+ charge transfer — less silk than Kashmir; brighter.

color_profiles.name: Padparadscha
color_profiles.cause: Combination of Cr3+ (pink) and iron-related (orange) color centers.

color_profiles.name: Star Sapphire
colors: black body with six-rayed white asterism
color_profiles.cause: Rutile (silk) needle inclusions in three crystallographic orientations producing asterism under cabochon cut.

color_profiles.name: Parti Sapphire
color_profiles.cause: Zoned distribution of different chromophores (Fe, Ti) during crystal growth.

visual_notes: Kashmir sapphire produces a 'velvety' blue that seems to glow from within — caused by fine silk inclusions scattering light. Star sapphire at its best shows a perfectly centered six-rayed star that moves over the cabochon surface. Padparadscha's salmon-pink-orange is unique and defies category — it must be evaluated in person to appreciate.

optical_effects.fluorescence: Blue sapphires typically inert to faint under UV; some Sri Lankan sapphires show orange fluorescence; yellow sapphires may fluoresce orange
optical_effects.asterism: Six-rayed star in star sapphire from three sets of rutile needles in basal plane
